summary: Shortly before delaying its four-day return-to-office due to lack of space, IRCC had to give up 12 floors of real estate in downtown Ottawa.

paragraphs: A few months before delaying its four-day return-to-office due to lack of space, the federal immigration department gave up 12 floors of real estate across two downtown Ottawa office buildings.

According to an August 2025 slideshow obtained by the Ottawa Citizen through the Access to Information Act, the department was required to return the floors to the government’s central property manager to meet its obligations under a federal plan to cut back on office space.

In support of the plan, Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) chose to release 10.5 floors at 300 Slater Street and 1.5 floors at 180 Kent Street back to Public Services and Procurement Canada (PSPC).
IRCC confirmed it completed the full release by the end of January.
But a few months later, the department had to delay the start of the government-wide four-day return-to-office mandate for most public servants because it didn’t have enough space to fit them.

The documents provide a glimpse into the internal gymnastics happening behind the scenes as federal departments attempt to bring workers back into the office more often, after trying for years to cut back on space.

headline: Return-to-office forcing pivot on plan to shed space
paragraphs: In recent months, PSPC has slowly reversed its messaging around a government plan to shed office space.
In 2024, PSPC committed to reducing the federal office portfolio by half over a decade, and Budget 2024 set aside $1.1 billion for the purpose.

The following year, a report from Canada’s auditor general highlighted that the government was only on pace to cut its real estate profile by about a third, as departments and agencies had been hesitant to sign off on reductions.

But in March 2026 , about a month after Treasury Board officials announced a four-day return-to-office for most public servants (up from three days), PSPC said its plan to offload space was being “adjusted.”
Then, in April, an internal PSPC analysis found more space may be added in cases where workstations are scarce. The department declined to provide that analysis.
Since then, multiple large federal departments have said they won’t be able to meet the latest remote work directive due to a lack of space, including Global Affairs Canada and IRCC.

In a May 7 memo obtained by the Ottawa Citizen, IRCC said all rank-and-file employees will be required in-office three days per week as of July 6, rather than the Treasury Board’s four-day mandate. Executives are already in five days a week, while managers in the department will follow the four-day requirement.
That memo was circulated about three months after IRCC gave up substantial downtown real estate at the request of PSPC.

In an email, PSPC spokesperson Michèle LaRose said 180 Kent is currently assigned to Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Canada while 300 Slater is assigned to the Department of National Defence, another organization facing space issues.
“The floors at both locations are being used to support evolving accommodation needs within the National Capital Region,” LaRose said.
“In response to increased on-site office presence, PSPC is managing its portfolio to ensure operational continuity.”
IRCC spokesperson Jeffrey MacDonald said in an email the cost associated with releasing the 12 floors came to about $58,000, most of which went toward moving furniture and IT equipment.
MacDonald said the department is working closely with PSPC and the Treasury Board Secretariat to ensure enough space is available for workers.
“IRCC will continue to review its space allocation on an ongoing basis, with the goal of moving to four days on site when space capacity allows,” MacDonald said.

headline: Directors lose offices in space reduction
paragraphs: According to the documents obtained under the Access to Information Act, the release of space required the “increased need for flexibility for employees working onsite.”
As a result, IRCC adjusted how it laid out its remaining office space after releasing the floors.

In a letter to other assistant deputy ministers, Murray Jones explained the changes: enclosed offices would be shared for short-term use, dedicated offices would be reserved for directors general and above, and directors would no longer have dedicated “closed door” offices.
Workstations could no longer be assigned on a one-to-one basis and would instead be unassigned as default, according to the presentation.

Treasury Board secretary Bill Matthews has said the public service will move away from desk hoteling and toward assigned seating where possible, though IRCC and other departments have since confirmed they will retain unassigned seating, despite the transition back into the office.

images.description: Federal government office building in downtown Ottawa.
images.caption: IRCC released 10.5 floors at the federal office building at 300 Slater Street, pictured here. Now, it's delaying the four-day return-to-office due to lack of space.

images.description: A copy of a presentation slide showing IRCC's plan to cut back office space.
images.caption: A snapshot of a presentation slide obtained through the Access to Information Act showing IRCC’s August 2025 plan to cut back on office space.

V1.title: IRCC gave up office space in January. Now, it can't accommodate RTO-4
